items.text: Purpose and guiding principles

SpecPad shall keep software documentation — requirements, verification tests, and architecture — as a version-controlled digital twin of the codebase, stored as JSON/markdown in the user's own git repository.
Git shall be the single history and merge layer: SpecPad shall store no edit history, authorship, or timestamps in the files, and nothing derived (counts, roll-ups) shall be persisted.
The skill and the visual editor shall obey one shared contract and enforce the same governance from a single source of truth, so the programmatic and human edit paths cannot disagree.
Two halves — skill and visual editor
A Claude Code skill shall create and maintain the documentation programmatically, generating stable ids and keeping the documents governance-clean as development proceeds.
Humans shall be able to edit the same documentation files visually that the skill maintains programmatically, with git as the single history and merge layer.

The visual editor shall be hosted and version-pinned (the schemaVersion maps to its URL path), and every published version path shall stay live forever so old documents always open in the editor they were authored against.
The documentation set
SpecPad shall capture software requirements (SRS) and verification tests (VTP), with every requirement traceably verified by at least one test.
Software requirements shall be able to trace upward to product requirements, supporting the design-controls trace matrix (user need → requirement → verification).
SpecPad shall capture software architecture as an arc42 document with embedded diagrams, kept alongside the requirements and co-versioned with them.
SpecPad shall capture detailed design (SDD) — the unit-level design that elaborates the architecture — as a companion document (IEC 62304 §5.4).
SpecPad shall capture a validation test plan whose tests confirm the product meets user needs / intended use, distinct from the verification (VTP) that confirms it meets the requirements.
SpecPad shall capture a usability / user-interface specification and assist a usability impact assessment per change, including a before/after report of affected workflows.
SpecPad shall maintain a third-party-components register aligned with SBOM standards (CycloneDX/SPDX) to satisfy SOUP/OTS and FDA cybersecurity bill-of-materials expectations.
SpecPad shall capture cybersecurity documentation (threat model, security architecture, controls) as a companion pillar, much of it derivable from the architecture and dependency manifests.
Change control and traceability
Every change to product behavior shall be traceable to an authorized unit of work (a job) and the commits that implemented it, so an auditor can answer "why does this exist and who approved it".

SpecPad shall provide an end-to-end trace chain — product requirement → software requirement → verification test → source code — with the source link derived from git commit trailers rather than a maintained matrix.
A release shall be a first-class, versioned design-control checkpoint: a full snapshot of all key documents plus the set of jobs that shipped in it, readable as release notes.
Validation tests shall trace to product requirements / user needs, completing the design-controls matrix (user need → validation) alongside requirement → verification.

SpecPad shall support traceability of risk control measures to requirements and system tests (IEC 62304 requirement ↔ system test ↔ risk control), without imposing a requirement-to-architecture matrix.
Regulatory fit and evidence export
SpecPad shall produce design-control evidence — requirements, tests, architecture, and releases captured at versioned checkpoints — suitable for export to an external quality system.

Regulatory classification shall be a configurable profile (IEC 62304 safety class and FDA documentation level), not a hard-coded scheme, so the same product serves current and future classification rules.
Non-medical projects shall be first-class: a lean generic profile shall provide the full value (requirements, tests, architecture, traceability) without any regulatory overhead, which is opt-in.
SpecPad shall export a regulatory evidence package — the versioned document set with per-job impact and traceability — into an external eQMS (the primary regulatory deliverable).
SpecPad shall generate release documents as Word/Office files rendered into a company template (with diagram images), suitable for an eQMS that expects document-level artifacts and tracked changes.
Adoption and onboarding
Installing SpecPad shall just work: a one-command setup shall scaffold the documents, install the change-control hook, and wire the working loop with no manual configuration.
SpecPad shall be adoptable on an existing codebase by drafting an initial requirements/test baseline from the code, for human ratification, mapped to existing tests.
SpecPad shall continuously reconcile the documentation against the code (drift detection / requirement audit), reporting gaps in both directions without destructive automatic edits.
SpecPad shall import an existing project's design history (SAD/SRS/VTP/releases), baselining everything at a version tag as "classic" documentation, after which full change controls apply.
SpecPad shall fit any git branching/development workflow: change attribution shall ride commit trailers and an active-job marker rather than dictating how teams branch, merge, or release.
Distribution and integrations
SpecPad shall be open-source and free at its core (MIT), distributed as an installable skill, with the regulated layer shipped as an opt-in add-on so unregulated teams pay nothing for it.

SpecPad shall ingest org-defined work items from external trackers (Jira/Confluence) at the moment work begins on a job, seeding the job and the first requirement/PRD drafts from that context — pull at job start, not a live sync.
Multi-repo devices shall be supported by per-repo SpecPad instances tied together at a system level through hand-authored version pinning (a device-master document), not by coupling the repositories.
